Back: A cause of an accident that makes a person or company legally responsible (liable) under the law.
A careless behavior that causes an accident, making a person or company legally accountable.
Failing to take proper care, which causes an accident and results in legal liability.
A type of responsibility (obligation-responsibility) focused on fulfilling duties and obligations in the future.
Requirements such as having specialist knowledge (competence) and a commitment to excellence in service delivery.
The relationship between professionals and society, requiring engineers to act responsibly so the public trusts their judgement.
A backward-looking responsibility (blame-responsibility) regarding past actions and whether obligations were met.
Negative outcomes caused by actions or inactions, which are the main focus of blame-responsibility.
The capacity to be held blame-responsible, raising the question of whether organizations like NASA can be held accountable like individuals.
The level or quality of service ordinarily provided by normally competent practitioners in that field.
Exercising the standard of reasonable care similar to other professionals in the same locality and circumstances.
The specific professionals involved in a project, whose personal accountability was questioned in the Columbia disaster.
The leadership team that refused to inspect the Columbia's wing or inform the astronauts of the danger until just before return.
The concept of holding an entire entity or company accountable for failures, rather than just individuals.
Fractured or diffused responsibility in groups where harm is produced by collective action or inaction.
A blind spot where we deceive ourselves as a means to protect ourselves.
A blind spot where people intentionally choose not to face the truth.
Failing to perceive something that is right in front of you, acting as a blind spot to responsibility.
Accepting problems or dangers as normal instead of remedying them, such as NASA's belief that foam strikes posed no real threat.
The exclusive concern to satisfy one's own interests even at the cost of others, which can blind us to professional responsibilities.
A form of ignorance where a person assumes that others' perspectives are similar to their own.
Focusing intensely on specific details or intricate knowledge while remaining blind to the bigger picture.
Losing one's sense of moral responsibility by blindly following orders, often fueled by hierarchical organizational structures.
Studies showing that two-thirds of volunteers would administer severe shocks to others if an authoritative figure promised to take responsibility.
An experiment demonstrating how individuals conform to group pressures, illustrated by comparing the lengths of lines.
A symptom of groupthink where the group believes they are immune to failure or harm.
A symptom of groupthink where individuals withhold their own dissenting opinions or concerns to fit in.
A symptom of groupthink involving keeping outside, dissenting views away from the group.
A symptom of groupthink where members falsely believe everyone in the group is in total agreement.
